prompt: portrait of extreme close-up of a software engineer in a dark server room, three-quarter view facing right, positioned in left third of frame, right side of frame empty and dark, warm light on face, cold blue fill from behind. two opposing forces visible in a single frame — one side warm/organic, one side cold/digital, a clear visual divide down the center. The environment is tech — screens, server lights, a wall of stacked monitors showing dense terminal outputs, but the human element is raw and physical. THE CENTRAL VISUAL: the subject holds a printed page of code up to their face, reading it, but the page is visibly blank on the side facing the camera — they are staring at nothing. This is the first thing the viewer sees. Everything else in the frame exists to support this detail.. macro lens, f/1.8 shallow depth of field, subject fills frame, intimate detail, pores and textures visible. The inconsistent element is in sharp focus at the center of the frame. The rest of the scene is slightly softer. The eye must hit the wrong detail first.. Visual tension: the viewer should feel pulled between two worlds that cannot coexist. Key visual detail: a sharp boundary line between the two sides — like light meeting shadow, or skin meeting circuit. the amber light of server rack LEDs in a dark room or a printed stack of code pages with red pen annotations on skin or floating in the air around the subject. warm orange key light on subject, cold blue fill light, cinematic color contrast, extremely blurred background bokeh, out-of-focus city lights, very dark, shallow depth of field f/1.4, contemplative, tension, duality, thoughtful, cinematic portrait. editorial documentary photography, shot on 35mm film, available light only, no post-production color grading, slight grain. brushed aluminum, matte black plastic, dusty glass, cable rubber, industrial concrete floors. Shot like a documentary photograph of a real workplace. Physical objects have weight, dust, and wear. Technology is infrastructure — racks, cables, screens — not magic. Available light only. No visual effects that could not exist in a real photograph.. 4:5 aspect ratio, no text or writing in the image, single subject focus
